title: Preventing and Addressing Family Violence Investment: Funded projects and their descriptions - Canada.ca
content:
## Preventing and Addressing Family Violence Investment
Learn about the projects that the Public Health Agency of Canada is funding or has funded to prevent family violence.
- Investing in projects that prevent family violence
- Current funded projects

## Investing in projects that prevent family violence
Through the Preventing Family Violence Investment program, the Public Health Agency of Canada supports projects that prevent and reduce the health impacts of family violence. This national grants and contributions program funds community-based initiatives that:
- prevent family violence
- improve health outcomes for survivors
Funded projects help to build the evidence base of effective interventions in this field. They reach children, youth, older adults and families affected by violence.
The investment program also supports projects that build the capacity of health professionals and service providers to work safely and effectively with survivors of family violence.
Learn more about family violence:
- Family violence and your health
- Get help: Family violence helplines and services
- Public       Health Agency of Canada funding opportunities
Centre for Sexuality Society
Family Ties : Building support for 2SLGBTQQIA+ children
Building support for 2SLGBTQQIA+ children
The Raising Rainbows project is delivering and testing support groups for parents and caregivers of gender creative and sexual minority children and youth. The groups give parents knowledge, skills and tools to build healthy family relationships, foster their children's healthy development and well-being while affirming and advocating for their 2SLGBTQQIA+ children.
University of Calgary
Growing, and Leveraging a Community of Practice to Address Teen/Youth Dating Violence in Canada
PREVNet (Promoting Relationships and Eliminating Violence Network) is facilitating a community of practice to enhance collaboration and consolidate learnings from youth dating violence prevention projects. By leveraging expertise from a diverse group of stakeholders, the project maximizes the overall impact of investments by the Public Health Agency of Canada to end gender-based violence.
University of Calgary
Fostering Violence Prevention and Well-being for Black Women, Families and Communities Project profile
Fostering Violence Prevention and Well-being for Black Women, Families and Communities
The University of Calgary's Faculty of Social Work, with partners, is adapting the Alberta Men's Network Training Program to prevent and address domestic violence in Black communities. The project enhances knowledge, leadership capacity and well-being for Black individuals, families and communities in Calgary and Toronto.
University of Calgary
The WiseGuyz Program: Scaling Up and Out
The WiseGuyz Program: Scaling Up and Out
WiseGuyz is a youth dating violence prevention and healthy relationships promotion program. It focuses on healthy relationships, consent, sexual health, representation of gender and sexuality in the media, and addresses the social pressures of being a guy. The program is being scaled up and delivered to 1,000 adolescent boys in schools and community-based organizations across Alberta and the Northwest Territories.
British Columbia - National
Community-Based Research Centre (CBRC)
Building Community Responses to 2SLGBTQIA+ Youth Dating Violence
CBRC is delivering and testing a health promotion intervention to address dating violence, gender-based violence and related risks and protective factors for queer and trans youth. The intervention will be delivered and tested in the Yukon, Alberta, Quebec and Nova Scotia in English and French.
British Columbia - Regional
Réseau-Femmes Colombie-Britannique
Jeunes Leaders des Relations Saines : Mise à l'échelle (French only)
Jeunes Leaders des Relations Saines : Mise à l'échelle (French only)
This project is scaling up the Jeunes Leaders des Relations Saines program to reach more than 1,500 youth living in minority language settings in 6 schools in British Columbia. Program activities will promote healthy and respectful relationships. The research protocol will increase the evidence on effective prevention practices by reaching more youth and allowing for longitudinal data collection.
British Columbia - Local
Victoria Sexual Assault Centre
SHIFTing Culture: Culturally-relevant dating violence prevention program for racialized youth About the Centre
SHIFTing Culture: Culturally-relevant dating violence prevention program for racialized youth
The Centre is adapting, delivering and testing Project Respect, a dating violence prevention program, to better address and support Black and racialized youth in Victoria. This intervention is youth-led, culturally safe and engages social action to create leadership and positive relationships. The focus is on giving youth the knowledge and skills to identify and intervene in the root causes of systemic and gender-based violence.
British Columbia - National
University of British Columbia
Promoting Substance Use Health Through Improved Services in the Gender-based Violence Sector
Substance Use Health Through Improved Services in the Gender-based Violence Sector
EQUIP Health Care at the University of British Columbia, with EVA-BC and partners in Ontario and New Brunswick, are developing and testing evidence-based resources and guidance to better address the intersections of gender-based violence and substance use. People with lived experience of substance use stigma, Indigenous communities and francophone organizations are co-designing effective policies and practice.
British Columbia - National
Supporting Survivors of Abuse and Brain Injury through Research (SOAR)
Building Awareness and Service Provider Capacity for Gender-Based Violence Traumatic Brain Injury in Canada
Resources and training are being developed to give health care and social service providers across Canada the knowledge and skills to safely support and provide comprehensive, multi-sectoral care to clients with gender-based violence-related brain injury. Novel, community-based interventions that support survivors are also being developed and tested.
Immigrant and Refugee Community Organization of Manitoba
Addressing the Gaps: Dating Violence Prevention for Racialized Refugee Youth Using an Intersectional/GBA+ Lens and Addressing Systemic Barriers
IRCOM is co-developing and testing a healthy relationships curriculum for youth aged 12 to 18 in racialized, newcomer, and refugee communities in Winnipeg. The intervention will be cultural safe, youth-centred and integrate a Gender-Based Analysis Plus (GBA+) lens. The focus is on  healthy and unhealthy relationships, communication, consent, boundaries, emotional well-being, and the impacts of substance use on relationships and decision-making.
Positive Discipline in Everyday Life
Preventing Child Maltreatment and Promoting Cultural Safety by Scaling up Positive Discipline in Everyday Parenting (PDEP)
The Canadian evidence-based parent support PDEP program is being adapted, scaled up and tested among 600 parents in Ontario, British Columbia and Manitoba. The goal is to update the program and disseminate it more widely, building a foundation for its sustainability in English and French.
University of Manitoba
Increasing Research Capacity to Address Evidence Gaps on Child Maltreatment and Child Welfare in Canada
Increasing Research Capacity to Address Evidence Gaps on Child Maltreatment and Child Welfare in Canada
The project focus is on increasing capacity to generate impactful research by developing knowledge, skills and connections between graduate students/trainees, knowledge users and scientific experts. The goal is to prevent and reduce the impact of child maltreatment, improve child welfare practices and make effective interventions accessible in various settings and situations in Canada.
Infinity Women Secretariat
Confidence through Culture for Healthy Relationships
Confidence through Culture for Healthy Relationships
Red River Métis culture as a foundation in delivering healthy youth relationship and violence prevention programming to 210 Métis youth in the 7 Manitoba Métis Federation regions across Manitoba is being explored and promoted. Specifically, the project is looking at how fostering a sense of belonging through culture can promote emotional well-being and build confidence and self-esteem in relationships.
Nova Scotia - Regional
Antigonish Women's Resource Centre and Sexual Assault Services Association
Healthy Relationships for Youth (HRY) Program
Healthy Relationships for Youth (HRY) Program
The HRY peer-facilitated program is being scaled up to reach 500 grade 9 students and then its impact is being tested. The curriculum explores topics that are relevant to all types of relationships, including how relationships can change, challenges that may arise and the intersections of different aspects of one's identity.
Government of Nunavut
Atii Angutiit: Call Line for Inuit Men
Through the Northern Wellness Agreement, a new support hotline is being developed for Inuit men. This hotline will give Inuit men in Nunavut a place to call, where they know they will find an understanding Inuk male who they can talk to. Counsellors will provide support to men who may be in crisis and feeling that they may harm someone or themselves, or who need personal support.
Nunavummi Disabilities Makinnasuaqtiit Society
Threads of Empowerment: Disabled Youth and Healthy Relationships in Nunavut Mandate: To achieve independence, self-determination and full citizenship for all Nunavummiut living with disability
A dating violence program tailored to the specific needs of disabled Inuit youth across Nunavut is being developed, implemented and tested. Through interactive workshops, participants are learning about boundaries, consent and communication, how to recognize signs of healthy and unhealthy relationships, and how to develop strategies for preventing dating violence in their communities.
Sapujjiniq ("protecting from harm")
Two family strengthening programs are being adapted and delivered in 6 communities across Nunavut for current unserved populations. Both interventions use interconnected, culturally specific programming to build knowledge and skills in parenting, communication, engagement with children, parent and child relationships, impacts of addiction, family dynamics and family routines.
University of Ottawa
Protection des enfants en contexte de violence conjugale (PEVC) (French only): Support and shelter resources for women who experience violence and their children
Protection des enfants en contexte de violence conjugale (PEVC) (French only):
Support and shelter resources for women who experience violence and their children
The PEVC model is being adapted, implemented and tested to increase knowledge of child protection policies and procedures among service providers and support individuals who experience intimate partner violence and their children across Quebec. Resources and training are being provided to 1,440 service providers in 120 shelters and support centres in Quebec.
Access Alliance Multicultural Health and Community Services
HEAL (Hubs of Expressive Arts for Life) to prevent and address family violence Project profile
HEAL (Hubs of Expressive Arts for Life) to prevent and address family violence
HEAL, a culturally safe, multi-faceted program aimed at preventing and addressing family violence, is being implemented and tested. This community-based project collaborates with vulnerable newcomer populations who are survivors or at risk of domestic violence, as co-researchers. The project develops expressive art-based interventions to address the health impacts of family violence and improve participants' well-being.
Promoting Positive and Healthy Youth Relationships
A healthy relationships promotion program is being adapted, delivered and tested among 500 youth who are at risk of dating violence in the Thunder Bay area. Youth-serving organizations are developing specialized resources for 200 service providers who will deliver the program.
CHAMPP4KIDS: Developing community-partnered, tailored models of care to promote positive parenting, thriving children and health equity
Two evidence-based programs (Parenting for Lifelong Health and Family Check-up) are being adapted, delivered and tested to address current gaps in parenting programs. Over 1,500 families in the Hamilton and Toronto regions will benefit from culturally response, relevant and accessible resources for service providers and caregivers of children aged 0 to 6.
Elizabeth Fry Toronto
Standing Strong: Trauma Recovery Through Empowerment
Standing Strong: Trauma Recovery Through Empowerment
The focus of this project is to prevent youth dating violence and support survivors' recovery, especially young women who have already experienced gender-based violence or family violence. The Trauma Recovery Empowerment Model, an evidence-based intervention, is being adapted to support youth survivors of trauma from underserved and at-risk populations.
Canadian Mothercraft Society
Breaking the Cycle (C-BTC) of Violence Project profile
Breaking the Cycle (C-BTC) of Violence
The initiative enhances access to an interpersonal violence group intervention for pregnant women and mothers of young children experiencing family violence in 15 communities across Canada. An evaluation of C-BTC will increase the evidence base for the program and its approaches, confirm its impacts over time, and identify the mechanisms of change observed among pregnant women and mothers who are engaged in the intervention.
Coaching Association of Canada
Support Through Sport (STS) for Youth Coaches
Support Through Sport (STS) for Youth Coaches
The STS program is being adapted, delivered and tested to help coaches address, prevent and intervene in gender-based violence and youth dating violence, and promote and model healthy relationships in and through sport. The project will reach 200 youth coaches from across Canada, including 2SLGBTQI+ youth, Black and racialized youth, and youth with a disability.
Legacy of Hope Foundation
Cultivating Respectful Connections: Indigenous Youth's Journey to Healthy Relationships
A youth dating violence prevention intervention for Indigenous youth across Canada is being developed, delivered and tested. The project includes developing an online dating violence prevention toolkit. Online workshops will be delivered to 300 Indigenous youth across Canada, providing an opportunity to engage with toolkit materials, create safe spaces for conversations and offer cultural teachings.
Les Femmes Michif Otipemisiwak
Ohpikihaawashow: Métis Reproductive Justice from Hospital to Home
Culturally relevant resources that support Métis families and health care providers, social workers and service providers involved in reproductive care are being created and tested. The project aims to support and engage Métis families who are either at risk or experiencing child welfare involvement while accessing reproductive care.
Students Commission of Canada
Be the Program, a dating violence intervention, is being delivered and tested among key youth populations in communities across Canada. Youth are discussing their needs and knowledge about healthy dating relationships and gaining skills on preventing dating violence for themselves and their peers. The intervention creates safer spaces to dive deeply into youth dating violence prevention through activities and peer-to-peer discussion.
An Evidenced-Based Health Promotion Intervention (iHEAL) for Women Experiencing IPV: Implementation in Diverse Contexts, Evaluation and Scale-Up Project profile
An Evidenced-Based Health Promotion Intervention (iHEAL) for Women Experiencing IPV: Implementation in Diverse Contexts, Evaluation and Scale-Up
The iHEAL program, a trauma-informed health-promotion intervention for women who have experienced intimate partner violence, is being delivered and tested in collaboration with researchers from 2 Canadian universities. Registered nurses are delivering the 6-month program in inclusive ways across community health settings in British Columbia, Ontario and New Brunswick. The evaluation focuses on sustaining benefits, organizational scalability and expanding beyond the initial partners.
University of Windsor
"Girls – Flip the Script with EAAA " across Canada
"Girls – Flip the Script with EAAA " across Canada
The EAAA program is being scaled up and tested for community-based organizations in British Columbia and Ontario. For girls aged 14 to 18, the program addresses girls' emotional and cognitive processes related to sexual violence victimization, increasing their skills to effectively use verbal and physical self-defence strategies.
University of Toronto
Co-developing evaluation mechanisms: Interrupting overrepresentation with culturally based interventions
The project will partner with 2 Indigenous Child and Family Well-Being Agencies and 1 Children's Aid Society to evaluate 4 culturally based health promotion interventions in Ontario. The project will reach 1,200 children and families receiving these interventions and will also work with service providers to support their unique needs.
University of Toronto
Implementing and testing RISE : Addressing a gap in community-based elder abuse response intervention Project profile
Implementing and testing RISE :
Addressing a gap in community-based elder abuse response intervention
RISE is an innovative community-based elder abuse response intervention. It integrates evidence-based approaches and modalities from other relevant fields, showing promising results with elder abuse survivors. The project involves implementing and testing this conceptually driven intervention with a goal to offer effective solutions to address elder abuse within the community context.
Iris is a health promotion intervention focused on sexual education with neurodivergent youth in community-based settings in Montreal. The project helps 450 neurodivergent youth aged 12 to 35 develop knowledge, skills and behaviours that promote equal, safe and consensual intimate relationships. The program addresses the types of relationships, the essential components for maintaining healthy relationships and key communication styles.
Université du Québec à Chicoutimi
Kaskinomatasowin: Sexual violence prevention and awareness Project profile
Kaskinomatasowin: Sexual violence prevention and awareness
The Kaskinomatasowin (transmitting through culture) community intervention sexual violence prevention program is aimed at preventing sexual violence among young people in Atikamekw Indigenous communities. As an extension of the Lantern|Awacic program tailored to Atikamekw culture, a culturally adapted module for parents is being developed to enhance parenting skills and prevent child sexual violence. The program targets over 200 parents from 3 Atikamekw Indigenous communities in Quebec.
Promoting healing and recovery of children and adolescents exposed to trauma: Culturally safe simulation-based virtual training for child welfare staff
The university is developing, implementing and testing a bilingual, simulation-based virtual training on trauma-informed care for child welfare staff to promote the healing and recovery of children and adolescents served by child welfare. The project is being delivered in partnership with multiple stakeholders, including child welfare agencies in Quebec, Ontario and Alberta, collaborators from various universities and people with lived experience.
Martin Family Initiative (MFI)
Culturally adapted resources and training for Indigenous family well-being
Culturally adapted resources and training for Indigenous family well-being
MFI is adapting and testing its health and family well-being resources to better support early childhood professionals in promoting positive parenting and understanding the effect that culturally appropriate education has on Indigenous early childhood professionals' capacity to support positive outcomes in families. The goal is to help reduce child maltreatment in the Indigenous communities they serve.
Centre d'expertise Marie-Vincent
The Centre d'expertise Marie-Vincent is delivering and evaluating the Lantern Program in Quebec. The program prevents sexual violence for children aged 0 to 5 through age-appropriate sexual health education and promotes safe relationships. It helps children develop skills on intimacy, privacy and boundaries. Professionals and parents are trained and provided with resources to promote safe, healthy relationships for young children.
Université de Québec à Montréal
The project will scale up and evaluate SPARX, a multi-component dating violence prevention program targeting students aged 14 to 16, peer supporters, school staff and parents/caregivers across Quebec. A Young Ambassadors initiative is being developed to promote the program in schools. Up to 375 facilitators are being trained to deliver the program and a community of practice brings them together to share knowledge.
Saskatchewan - Local
Family Service Saskatoon Inc.
Youth empowered: Building healthy relationships
The project is delivering and testing a health promotion intervention for Saskatoon youth aged 10 to 22. It aims to equip youth with the skills to foster healthy relationships and prevent youth dating violence. The focus is on youth who have experienced trauma in their early years, as well as newcomer, racialized and 2SLGBTQI+ youth, and youth who have experienced gender-based violence.
University of Calgary
Changing Contexts: Art of the Nudge Project profile
Changing Contexts: Art of the Nudge
Shift! The Project to End Domestic Violence at the University of Calgary partnered with the Calgary Police Service to deliver and test the Art of the Nudge. This project promoted gender-equitable, non-violent behaviours in up to 200 men within male-dominated environments. The goal was to reduce bias and sexual harassment and promote inclusion in physical, social and organizational settings.
University of Calgary
ConnectED Parents : Moving to virtual interventions to engage parents in the prevention of adolescent dating violence Project profile
Moving to virtual interventions to engage parents in the prevention of adolescent dating violence
Shift! The Project to End Domestic Violence has scaled up and tested the ConnectED Parents program to address gender inequities and norms. It has built capacity in parents of young people aged 10 to 20 in Calgary to teach healthy relationships. Through educational text messages, it enhanced parents' skills, fostered a healthy social climate and supported peer-to-peer capacity-building among parent groups.
British Columbia - Local
DIVERSEcity community resources society
Stories  of Strength : A community-led safety planning and action research  initiative to address and prevent family violence in immigrant and refugee  families Project  profile
Stories  of Strength :
A community-led safety planning and action research  initiative to address and prevent family violence in immigrant and refugee  families
The project adapted and evaluated the Signs of Safety program for multigenerational safety planning to address and prevent family violence. The project promoted the strengths and strategies of diverse cultural communities in Surrey and surrounding areas. Kitchen table dialogues were organized with families to enhance safety behaviours. Workshops were held to share "stories of strength" and create culturally responsive toolkits.
British Columbia - National
Centre of Excellence for Women's Health
Strong Women: Reducing the Impact of Intimate Partner Violence and Substance Use on Women's Health Project profile
Strong Women: Reducing the Impact of Intimate Partner Violence and Substance Use on Women's Health
The Centre  delivered and tested the Strong Women intervention, in partnership with anti-violence and substance use services across Canada, to support women who experienced both intimate partner violence and substance use. The virtual social support group has been pilot tested at 2 B.C.-based agencies and at 6 anti-violence and substance use service provider agencies in Alberta, Ontario and New Brunswick.
World Health Organization (WHO)
Parenting support to prevent maltreatment and enhance parent-child relationships: A training package for the selection, adaptation and scaling up of evidence-based interventions Project profile
Parenting support to prevent maltreatment and enhance parent-child relationships: A training package for the selection, adaptation and scaling up of evidence-based interventions
Building on PHAC-funded parenting intervention materials, training packages have been developed to build the capacity of government and community-based organizations to identify, adapt and scale up evidence-based parenting interventions for preventing child maltreatment.
Strengthening the Health Sector Response to Child Maltreatment Responding to child maltreatment: a clinical handbook for health professionals
Strengthening the Health Sector Response to Child Maltreatment
Responding to child maltreatment: a clinical handbook for health professionals
Evidence-based guidance and training has been developed and tested to build the capacity of health professionals to recognize and respond to child maltreatment across Canada and internationally. Resources such as the handbook will help health professionals identify child maltreatment in their day-to-day practice and communicate safely with children and caregivers about abuse.
New Brunswick - National
University of New Brunswick
The AIM Study Project profile
The Muriel McQueen Fergusson Centre for Family Violence Research has adapted and evaluated a 13-week advocacy intervention for women in midlife and older who experience intimate partner violence. The project has reached up to 60 older women in Nova Scotia, New Brunswick and Prince Edward Island. It offered tailored interventions, including safety planning, information on violence cycles, community and legal resources, and helping women develop future goals and strategies.
Nova Scotia - National
Dalhousie University
Safety and Repair Project profile
Safety and Repair, a 10-week online family-violence prevention program, has been adapted, implemented and tested in rural and remote communities in Atlantic Canada (Nova Scotia, New Brunswick, Prince Edward Island, and Newfoundland and Labrador). The goal was to help individuals who use violence within families build safer relationships.
We Are Family: Challenging the roots of 2SLGBTQQIA+ family rejection and violence via early parent intervention on femmephobia
An interactive curriculum on femmephobia (the regulation, devaluation and denigration of femininity) has been developed and tested for existing parent support programs, an online workbook and a website for parents and caregivers. The project has reached up to 600 participants in Ontario and aimed to promote healthy environments for 2SLGBTQQIA+ youth by reducing femmephobia-related prejudice and harm in families and communities.
Building Opportunities for Women (B.O.W.) Project profile
Building Opportunities for Women (B.O.W.)
The 12-week B.O.W. project has been delivered and tested in Northern Ontario. Groups of 10 to 15 women explored the roots of gender-based violence, addressed their own sense of self-esteem and vulnerabilities, and identified their future education and training needs.
Leveraging TVIPA to support individuals who have experienced family violence: A community-based participatory approach Project profile
Leveraging TVIPA to support individuals who have experienced family violence: A community-based participatory approach
The Health and Wellness Equity Research Group led a community-based project to co-develop, deliver and evaluate trauma and violence-informed physical activity (TVIPA) programming at 3 culturally diverse sites in Ottawa, Toronto and Vancouver. The goal was to improve access to TVIPA, foster social connections, enhance community cohesion, and support the overall health and well-being of women and children affected by family violence.
Central Toronto Youth Services
A community of practice is being established and hosted for professionals and service providers working with 2SLGBTQIA+ children and youth across Canada. It will create a support network for 2SLGBTQIA+ workers and allies through regular practice calls, webinars and resources to advocate for policy and practice changes. The project is also identifying opportunities for direct support to frontline projects in communities most in need.
Moving On : Digital Empowerment and Literacy Skills for Survivors (MODELSS) Project profile
Digital Empowerment and Literacy Skills for Survivors (MODELSS)
A digital literacy program has been created for survivors of family violence in transitional housing. Through workshops, the program empowered survivors by providing online safety information, educational content to prevent technology-facilitated violence and digital literacy skills.
Building Capacity to Prevent Gender-based Violence - Development and Evaluation of Evidence-Based Tools for Canadian Educators
The project built educators' capacity by developing, delivering and piloting evidence-based training on gender, gender-based violence and prevention, with a focus on incorporating youth voices in all tools and resources. These resources have been shared with educators through a national social marketing campaign, developed in collaboration with national partners. The tools' utility, feasibility and acceptability have also been tested.
Knowledge Hub Project profile
Led by the Centre for Research and Education on Violence Against Women, the Knowledge Hub hosted a community of practice that connected and enhanced the work of intervention research projects focused on family violence prevention. It consolidated learning, built capacity for trauma-informed health promotion and facilitated collaboration among practitioners. The Hub hosted a web platform to share knowledge and identified common ways to measure project outcomes.
University of Windsor
"Girl, you got this!": Adapting an evidence-based program to help teens girls effectively resist sexual dating violence
The Enhanced Assess, Acknowledge, Act (EAAA) program has been adapted for high school girls aged 14 to 17. This evidence-based sexual violence prevention intervention has been delivered in 3 universities in Alberta and Ontario.
2018 to 2026 (on pause 2021 and 2022)
Université de Montréal
The study of the Espace Parents (French only) initiative: An intervention aimed at promoting parenting skills and preventing family violence against children among newcomers Project profile
The study of the Espace Parents (French only) initiative: An intervention aimed at promoting parenting skills and preventing family violence against children among newcomers
The Espace Parents program, which promotes parenting skills and aims to prevent child maltreatment, has been delivered to newly arrived immigrant families. The 2-part program consists of 9 group sessions and 2 targeted sessions for harder-to-reach populations. It sought to reach over 300 parents from 8 partner community organizations in the Greater Montreal area.
Centre d'expertise Marie-Vincent
Compass Project profile
The Centre d'expertise Marie-Vincent has developed and evaluated a program to prevent sexual violence and promote egalitarian relationships for children aged 6 to 12 and supportive adults in their lives, by adapting the Lantern program. It created prevention and training tools for over 600 community professionals from more than 200 organizations across Quebec. The program evaluation has helped identify best practices for trauma-informed sexual violence prevention.
Université du Québec à Trois-Rivières
STEP project : A prenatal program for survivors of complex trauma aiming to prevent family violence and to promote maternal and child health Project profile
A prenatal program for survivors of complex trauma aiming to prevent family violence and to promote maternal and child health
The STEP (Supporting the Transition to and Engagement  in Parenthood) program has been scaled up, adapted and tested in 4 Quebec  regions. This trauma-informed prenatal program for survivors of complex trauma  promotes maternal health and child development. STEP has also been adapted to  meet the needs of populations disproportionately affected by family violence,  including Indigenous communities, cultural minorities and individuals with  psychiatric disorders.
Université de Sherbrooke
Acknowledging and Responding to Mistreatment in CARegiving (ARMCAR) Project profile
Acknowledging and Responding to Mistreatment in CARegiving (ARMCAR)
The Research Chair on Mistreatment of Older Adults has developed ARMCAR, a bilingual, online, multicomponent training program to help prevent and manage mistreatment situations in a caregiving context involving persons aged 50 and older. The program has been offered to informal caregivers and employees and volunteers of Quebec organizations that offer direct support.
Council of Yukon First Nations
Yukon First Nation Violence Prevention Program Project profile
Yukon First Nation Violence Prevention Program
The project aimed to develop, deliver and test a violence prevention program in Yukon by and for Yukon First Nations.
Luna Child and Youth Advocacy Centre
Being Trauma Aware : Making a difference in the lives of children and youth
Being Trauma Aware :
Making a difference in the lives of children and youth
Being Trauma Aware, a trauma-informed training program exploring links between childhood maltreatment, brain development, substance use and mental health, was developed and tested. Interactive online modules helped Alberta service providers safely support children affected by trauma, fostering integrated practices across sectors to better serve and address the needs of children and youth.
Luna Child and Youth Advocacy Centre
Being Trauma Aware Phase 2
Being Trauma Aware Phase 2
Being Trauma Aware was expanded to improve understanding of child maltreatment effects among service providers. Participants across Canada were trained to deliver safe care to survivors of trauma and abuse, enhancing resources and knowledge on addressing child maltreatment.
University of Calgary
The WiseGuyz Program : Preventing trauma and domestic violence through gender-based healthy relationships promotion
The WiseGuyz Program :
Preventing trauma and domestic violence through gender-based healthy relationships promotion
The WiseGuyz Program was delivered to Grade 9 boys to address harmful gender norms. It promoted inclusive masculinities and reduced dating violence through school-based workshops. WiseGuyz built evidence to scale its transformative approach to fostering healthier relationships among boys in diverse settings.
British Columbia - Local
Victoria Sexual Assault Centre
Project Respect : Preventing dating violence by SHIFTing culture
Preventing dating violence by SHIFTing culture
The project was delivered in 7 schools to foster consent culture and challenge gender stereotypes. It trained youth leaders and staff, creating inclusive environments and culturally relevant programs for Indigenous students. Workshops emphasized healthy boundaries and collaborative action to prevent dating violence and gender-based harm.
British Columbia - Local
Dating Safe , a dating violence prevention program
Dating Safe , a dating violence prevention program
YWCA Vancouver delivered this school-based program for youth, as part of the province's physical health education curriculum. It taught skills to navigate diverse relational challenges, addressing dating violence in ethnically diverse schools in Vancouver and Surrey. The program fostered inclusive, supportive environments for youth from all backgrounds.
British Columbia - Regional
BC Society of Transition Houses
Reaching out with Yoga Project profile
Reaching out with Yoga
In partnership with Yoga Outreach, the project delivered trauma-informed yoga programs in shelters and transition houses across the province. Women and children who experienced family violence improved their physical and mental health. Staff were also trained to integrate yoga into their practice, using it to address vicarious trauma and enhance overall service quality.
British Columbia - Regional
Multilingual Orientation Service Association for Immigrant Communities (MOSAIC)
Enhancing Healthy Relationships Program (EHRP) : Multicultural youth and safe relationships
Enhancing Healthy Relationships Program (EHRP) :
Multicultural youth and safe relationships
MOSAIC designed EHRP, a teen dating violence prevention program tailored to immigrant, refugee and racialized families. Workshops for youth and caregivers addressed cultural and generational gaps in understanding relationships. Community consultations informed program design, creating resources to foster intergenerational learning and healthy, culturally respectful relationships.
British Columbia - Regional
University of British Columbia
Taking Pride : Fostering healthy relationships among LGBTQ2+ youth in British Columbia
Fostering healthy relationships among LGBTQ2+ youth in British Columbia
The University of British Columbia, Stigma and Resilience Among Vulnerable Youth Centre and McCreary Centre Society collaborated to develop, implement and evaluate Taking Pride, a healthy relationship program for sexual and gender minority youth throughout British Columbia. The program was co-developed with LGBTQ2+ youth and consisted of 5 20-minute modules that were based on the information-motivation-behavioural skills model of health behaviour change.
British Columbia - Regional
Réseau-Femmes Colombie- Britannique
Jeunes Leaders des Relations Saines (French only)
Jeunes Leaders des Relations Saines (French only)
This school-based violence prevention program for francophone youth (grades 9 to 12) was developed, delivered and tested in minority linguistic settings in 4 francophone schools in British Columbia. The research focus was on understanding the perspectives of both youth and caregivers and explored ways to involve them in the research process.
"UMatter" Stop Youth Dating Violence Project
Dating violence prevention programs tailored to Indigenous children, youth and young adults were created. The programs addressed historical and relational contexts to promote healthy relationships. Delivered in Winnipeg, the initiative engaged First Nations communities through peer-led training and capacity building, fostering safer relational dynamics across diverse age groups.
Ode'imaa zhigo ode'imaa (Heart to Heart)
In collaboration with Ma Mawi and RESOLVE, the project led a culturally adapted dating violence prevention program for Indigenous teens in Winnipeg. Using youth advisory input, the initiative integrated Indigenous practices and fostered community-based supports. It promoted healthy relationships and addressed systemic and cultural challenges within Indigenous communities.
New Brunswick - Regional
Beausejour Family Crisis Resource Centre
Kids Club : A health promotion program for children exposed to family violence in New Brunswick Project profile
A health promotion program for children exposed to family violence in New Brunswick
A Kids Club and Moms' Empowerment program was developed for children aged 6 to 12 and their mothers exposed to family violence. Targeting rural francophone and newcomer communities in Fredericton and Beauséjour, the project aimed to improve outcomes for child witnesses of family violence. Findings will be shared provincially to promote best practices across New Brunswick.
Positive Discipline in Everyday Life
Positive Discipline in Everyday Parenting (PDEP): A trauma-and-violence-informed approach to ending child maltreatment
Positive Discipline in Everyday Parenting (PDEP): A trauma-and-violence-informed approach to ending child maltreatment
PDEP aimed to change parental beliefs and emotional reactions known to precipitate physical and emotional punishment of children. The project reached 1,260 parents among diverse communities in Canada, including Indigenous families, newcomers, young parents, grandparents, parents involved in the child welfare system and parents with low literacy.
Northwest Territories - Regional
FOXY: Strengthening the health of Northern and Indigenous youth experiencing teen dating violence in the Northwest Territories Project profile
FOXY: Strengthening the health of Northern and Indigenous youth experiencing teen dating violence in the Northwest Territories
A school-based program was developed and tested in the Northwest Territories, with Indigenous girls, to address teen dating violence and promote healthy relationships, mental health and sexual health. Using drama, visual arts and cultural practices, the project empowered youth to navigate relationships safely while fostering resilience and self-expression.
Nova Scotia - Regional
Antigonish Women's Resource Centre and Sexual Assault Services Association
Healthy Relationships for Youth (HRY) : Making the case for youth facilitated dating violence prevention programs
Healthy Relationships for Youth (HRY) :
Making the case for youth facilitated dating violence prevention programs
The peer-facilitated violence prevention initiative was delivered to Grade 9 students. Targeting rural, Mi'kmaq, African Nova Scotian and newcomer communities across the province, it gave youth the skills to build healthy relationships. An evaluation process guided its improvement and broader dissemination.
The Qaujigiarttiit Health Research Centre
Inunnguiniq Parenting/Childrearing Program Project profile
Inunnguiniq Parenting/Childrearing Program
The program was adapted for Inuit caregivers facing challenges like substance use and justice system involvement. Rooted in Inuit values, it addressed intergenerational trauma and strengthened families, promoting holistic wellness, positive parenting and healthier connections for children and caregivers in Nunavut.
Shape your Life : Trauma-informed boxing for survivors of family violence Project profile
Trauma-informed boxing for survivors of family violence
A boxing program was delivered to cis and trans women survivors of family violence. This trauma-informed, non-contact approach empowered participants to reclaim control of their bodies while improving physical and mental health. The program created a supportive environment where survivors could build resilience and confidence.
Boost Child and Youth Advocacy Centre
Pep Talk: Intermediate
Pep Talk: Intermediate
The Pep Talk Intermediate program was delivered and evaluated in Toronto public and Catholic schools. Grade 9 students were taught how to build healthy relationships, identify unhealthy dynamics and prevent dating violence. The goal was to foster safer, violence-free youth relationships in diverse communities.
Boost Child and Youth Advocacy Centre
Sole Expression : Trauma-informed dance intervention for youth
Trauma-informed dance intervention for youth
The Centre developed and evaluated Sole Expression, a trauma-informed hip-hop dance intervention for youth who have experienced violence. Participants explored movement to relieve tension, rebuild confidence and regain control of their physical and mental well-being, reducing trauma symptoms and fostering personal empowerment.
Covenant House Toronto
P.E.A.C.E. Project: Peer Education And Connection through Empowerment Project profile
P.E.A.C.E. Project: Peer Education And Connection through Empowerment
A peer-led program was developed for youth aged 16 to 24 in Toronto who experienced sexual exploitation and homelessness. Participants learned self-healing, safety planning and leadership skills through activities like mindfulness, arts and physical fitness. Young women were empowered to achieve their goals and rebuild their lives.
Kawartha Sexual Assault Centre
Building internal resilience through horses Project profile
Building internal resilience through horses
The Centre delivered and tested an intervention combining equine-assisted learning, arts and psychoeducation to support girls aged 13 to 18 who are survivors of child maltreatment. Interaction with horses helped participants build resilience, confidence and self-awareness, creating a transformative experience for young women in Peterborough.
Planned Parenthood Ottawa
Building healthy relationships: Preventing teen dating violence through skills-based education
Building healthy relationships: Preventing teen dating violence through skills-based education
Classroom workshops were held to reduce teen dating violence among students in grades 8 and 9 in Ottawa schools and communities. Supported by York University and informed by extensive engagement with students and caregivers in a safer, violence-free learning environment, the program increased youth awareness of healthy relationships.
Thunder Bay District Health Unit
Youth Violence Prevention Project
Youth Violence Prevention Project
The Fourth R program was adapted to fit Northern Ontario's context. Teachers and stakeholders were trained to prevent teen dating violence and promote healthy relationships for students in grades 7 to 9. Delivered in classrooms, the program aimed to build resilience and emotional skills while addressing the unique social dynamics of the Thunder Bay region.
MindUP for Young Children Project profile
MindUP for Young Children
MindUP, a trauma-informed program teaching self-regulation, empathy and emotional skills, was delivered in schools and community settings in London, Ontario. It targeted young children exposed to family violence, aiming to improve developmental outcomes and reduce stress. Educators and facilitators received specialized training in trauma-informed care.
Women's Health in Women's Hands
Flourish Project profile
The project engaged survivors of female genital mutilation/cutting (FGM/C), community leaders and service providers to raise awareness and improve care. Arts-based workshops informed resources for survivors of FGM/C. A network of community champions was formed to address the harms of FGM/C and promote culturally sensitive support systems in the Greater Toronto Area.
Sharing in the Healing Journey
Sharing in the Healing Journey
Two expressive arts-based programs (Here to Help and Adventures in Sharing) were implemented and evaluated in Scarborough, Toronto, for children aged 4 to 16 and their mothers who have experienced intimate partner violence. The project helped caregivers and children manage emotions, strengthen resilience and develop coping strategies to reduce re-traumatization.
Trauma Informed Development Education (TIDE) project
Trauma Informed Development Education (TIDE) project
YWCA Toronto implemented the TIDE project to advance trauma-informed practices organization-wide. This initiative improved health and well-being for women experiencing family violence and enhanced support for staff coping with trauma. The cultural shift fostered a safer, more inclusive environment for both clients and employees.
Shape your Life – Youth Project profile
Shape your Life – Youth
In partnership with Mentoring Junior Kids Organization, a trauma-informed boxing program was developed for youth aged 7 to 18 affected by family violence. The program trained youth coaches and supported over 600 participants in improving physical and mental health, fostering resilience and promoting leadership development in Ontario and Alberta communities.
Canadian Paediatric Society
Trauma-informed pediatric care: Designing an approach informed by children, youth and families
Evidence-based resources and guidance were developed to equip pediatric health professionals in providing trauma-informed care. The project, located in Ottawa, Ontario, conducted a scoping review and engaged with children, youth and families with lived experiences to inform the development of bilingual resources.
Canadian Public Health Association
Preventing Youth Dating Violence: Building Capacity for Comprehensive Sexuality Education in Canada
Preventing Youth Dating Violence: Building Capacity for Comprehensive Sexuality Education in Canada
A sexuality education curriculum was adapted and delivered in schools and community settings. Reaching youth aged 12 to 19, it taught gender equity, healthy relationships and consent. The project aimed to build skills for navigating relationships free from violence while enhancing educators' ability to deliver impactful lessons in 6 locations.
Child Development Institute
Preventing Violence Behaviour: Implementing a national SNAP Community of Practice
The project hosted a community of practice among organizations that deliver the Stop Now And Plan (SNAP) program across the country. The community of practice reached up to 900 SNAP-trained clinicians, educators and Elders in more than 100 SNAP affiliate sites across Canada. This included other community key stakeholders, such as police, child welfare, community-based organizations and government.
Gender affirming adults: Parents and caregivers supporting their kids' gender identities
Gender affirming adults: Parents and caregivers supporting their kids' gender identities
Egale Canada adapted and evaluated guidelines for parents and caregivers of 2SLGBTQQIA+ youth, focusing on mental health, gender diversity and creating affirming spaces. The project reached up to 400 parents and caregivers, providing practical information to support children's gender identities and well-being at home, in school and when accessing care. The goal was to help parents foster safer, more nurturing relationships with their transgender and gender-diverse children.
Researching the Impact of Service provider Education ( RISE ): The VEGA Project
McMaster University evaluated the VEGA family violence education resources by engaging 500 health care professionals. The project improved provider skills in identifying and addressing family violence, tailoring tools to meet accreditation standards. It strengthened sector capacity to support survivors in 3 provinces.
Development of pan-Canadian public health guidance on family violence (VEGA): The VEGA Project
Development of pan-Canadian public health guidance on family violence (VEGA):
Led by the Preventing Violence Across the Lifecourse (PreVAiL) research network, McMaster University developed the Violence, Evidence, Guidance and Action (VEGA) pan-Canadian public health guidance, which included guidance documents, protocols, curricula and tools for health professionals. The goal was to better equip them to provide safe and appropriate care for victims of violence.
Building, Growing and Leveraging a Community of Practice to Address Teen and Youth Dating Violence in Canada Project profile
Building, Growing and Leveraging a Community of Practice to Address Teen and Youth Dating Violence in Canada
Queen's University's Promoting Relationships and Eliminating Violence Network (PREVNet) facilitated a community of practice to enhance collaboration and consolidate learning from the teen and youth dating violence prevention projects funded by the Public Health Agency of Canada.
Boys and Girls Club of Canada
Bounce Back League (BBL) Project profile
Bounce Back League (BBL)
The project developed and evaluated the Bounce Back League, a trauma-informed sports program for children aged 8 to 12 to promote resilience and improve mental and physical health. It combined sports drills, team activities and staff training to create safe, inclusive environments. Implemented in 13 clubs, BBL helps youth build life skills and navigate challenges.
Boys and Girls Clubs of Canada (BGC)
Preventing teen dating violence: Engaging BGC youth in building safe and healthy relationships Respect2Connect
Preventing teen dating violence: Engaging BGC youth in building safe and healthy relationships
The Respect2Connect, a community-based dating violence prevention program for grades 7 to 9, was developed and evaluated. It addressed dating violence risk factors in vulnerable communities and taught youth relationship skills. By leveraging club environments, it influenced how young people experience relationships, promoting safety and resilience nationwide.
Canadian Mothercraft Society
Building connections: A group intervention for mothers and children experiencing violence in relationships Project profile
Building connections: A group intervention for mothers and children experiencing violence in relationships
A group intervention to support mothers and children affected by family violence was delivered and tested. The program built parenting skills, self-esteem and relationship skills. Delivered through community programs, it included staff training to ensure effective responses, improving outcomes for families across Canada facing trauma and violence.
Canadian Women's Foundation
Building the field of teen healthy relationships programming in Canada
Building the field of teen healthy relationships programming in Canada
This project created networks of professionals and agencies to prevent teen dating violence. Indigenous working groups and regional hubs were established to directly engage 50 organizations and reach 750 more organizations through collaborative strategies to foster healthy relationships among youth.
Child Development Institute
Safe and Understood: Helping children who experience domestic violence Project profile
Safe and Understood: Helping children who experience domestic violence
Two programs (Caring Dads and Mothers in Mind) to improve outcomes for young children exposed to family violence were scaled up and evaluated in Ontario, Quebec and New Brunswick. The interventions improved parenting skills, stress management and self-esteem, fostering healthier family dynamics.
Child Welfare League of Canada
The Strength of Families and Connection : Child safety and wellbeing during COVID-19 response and recovery
The Strength of Families and Connection :
Child safety and wellbeing during COVID-19 response and recovery
Evidence-based training and resources were provided to strengthen the capacity of child welfare workers, service providers and caregivers during COVID-19. This nationwide initiative improved the prevention and response to child maltreatment, addressing the unique challenges posed by the pandemic and promoting child safety.
Coaching Association of Canada
Support Through Sport : Preventing and addressing gender-based violence in sport
Support Through Sport :
Preventing and addressing gender-based violence in sport
A Support Through Sport toolkit and training were provided to 6,000 coaches to address gender-based violence in sport. Resources emphasized bystander empowerment and fostering safe environments. Shared with 82 national sport organizations, the tools aimed to create inclusive, respectful cultures in Canadian sports.
Promoting Healthy Families : A Canadian evaluation of Triple P
Promoting Healthy Families :
A Canadian evaluation of Triple P
Three parenting interventions (Triple P, Circle of Security-Parenting and Parenting Under Pressure) were evaluated. The programs improved family relationships, parenting confidence and child behaviour; supported families facing challenges like substance use and violence; and enhanced family well-being across Canada.
Teen Dating Violence for Educators : Supporting educators' capacity to prevent dating violence
Teen Dating Violence for Educators :
Supporting educators' capacity to prevent dating violence
Queen's University's Promoting Relationships & Eliminating Violence Network (PREVNet) developed training for educators to deliver relationship curriculums to prevent dating violence. Models were created to enhance educator confidence in recognizing and addressing gender-based violence, equipping schools across Canada with tools to foster safe, healthy environments.
Students Commission of Canada
Be the Program, a youth-led dating violence prevention program, was developed and evaluated in 15 communities. Participants explored the impacts of gender inequality, toxic masculinity and media on relationships. By promoting social action, the program empowered youth to create safer, inclusive spaces and challenge harmful relational norms nationwide.
iHEAL : A Canadian health and safety mobile app for women experiencing intimate partner violence
A Canadian health and safety mobile app for women experiencing intimate partner violence
A free mobile app, iHEAL, was developed to support Canadian women who have experienced intimate partner violence. The app, made and hosted in Canada, is an interactive, evidence-based resource available in English and French. Relevant resources and personalized information helped women address the impacts of abuse on their health and well-being.
iHEAL in Context: Testing the effectiveness of a health promotion intervention for women who have experienced intimate partner violence Project profile
iHEAL in Context: Testing the effectiveness of a health promotion intervention for women who have experienced intimate partner violence
Intervention for Health Enhancement and Living (iHEAL), a health promotion intervention supporting survivors of intimate partner violence, was developed and tested. Over 6 months, community nurses partnered with women to improve safety, health and connections. The project advanced trauma-informed care, complementing existing community services and enhancing survivor well-being.
InterRAI child and youth mental health trauma-informed care project Project profile
InterRAI child and youth mental health trauma-informed care project
The effects of trauma-informed practices were assessed on children aged 4 to 18 affected by family violence using innovative health tools. Through evidence-based interventions, the project improved mental health care and promoted collaboration and service integration across organizations, enhancing outcomes for children and youth.
RISE-R: Resilience and Inclusion through Strengthening and Enhancing Relationships
RISE-R: Resilience and Inclusion through Strengthening and Enhancing Relationships
The Healthy Relationships Plus (HRP) program was adapted for underserved groups, including LGBTQ2+, Indigenous and newcomer youth in Ontario, Alberta and the Northwest Territories. Educators and health professionals were trained to deliver HRP programs tailored to local contexts.
Supporting the Health of Survivors of Family Violence in Family Law Proceedings
Supporting the Health of Survivors of Family Violence in Family Law Proceedings
The capacity of 15,000 professionals in health, violence prevention and family law sectors was enhanced. Through specialized training and cross-sector collaboration, efforts to protect the health and safety of survivors of family violence and those at risk were strengthened, fostering integrated support systems and improved outcomes.
Women's Centre for Social Justice
Cross-Sectoral Solutions: Strengthening Community Capacity to Address the 'Parallel Pandemic' of Intimate Partner Violence-Related Traumatic Brain Injury Through a Survivor-Led Support Intervention
Cross-Sectoral Solutions: Strengthening Community Capacity to Address the 'Parallel Pandemic' of Intimate Partner Violence-Related Traumatic Brain Injury Through a Survivor-Led Support Intervention
In collaboration with community partners, a trauma-informed, multisectoral intervention for women survivors of intimate partner violence with brain injury (IPV-TBI) was adapted, piloted and tested. It aimed to create a national "blueprint" for coordinated IPV-TBI services, sensitive to diverse identities and geographic contexts. Piloted in British Columbia, Ontario and Quebec, the project built community capacity and established pathways for multisector care referral and scalability.
Speak OUT: 2SLGBTQI Youth Dating Violence
Speak OUT: 2SLGBTQI Youth Dating Violence
A consent-focused dating violence prevention program for 2SLGBTQI youth was created and tested using an innovative hackathon approach. Teams of youth and experts designed arts-based interventions tailored to community needs. Tested in 5 Ontario communities, the program expanded knowledge sharing and empowered youth to foster healthier, safer relationships.
University of Toronto
Building Competencies, Building Capacity: LGBTQ2+ Focused Trauma-Informed Care
Building Competencies, Building Capacity: LGBTQ2+ Focused Trauma-Informed Care
An intervention to improve LGBTQ2+ cultural competency among trauma service providers was developed and tested. Ontario providers were trained to deliver inclusive care to LGBTQ2+ survivors. The project served as a model for scaling the program nationally.
Park Extension Youth Organization
Arts et contes en famille
Arts et contes en famille
A community arts intervention service for immigrant families in the Park-Extension neighbourhood of Montreal, Quebec, was developed and tested. Through workshops based on storytelling and artistic creation, the program aimed to reduce parental stress and improve family dynamics to prevent violence. The second part of the project focused on disseminating knowledge through an intervention guide and information capsules.
PortraitX was an arts-based program that addressed gender-based violence. Delivered in Montreal, it used an interactive platform to teach self-awareness, empathy and communication. It reached youth from diverse cultural, racial and LGBTQ2+ backgrounds, fostering understanding and skills for healthier relationships.
Canadian Association of Midwives
Recognize and Respond: Building midwives' capacity to address family violence
Recognize and Respond: Building midwives' capacity to address family violence
Over 440 midwives were trained to identify and respond to family violence. Partnering with the National Aboriginal Council of Midwives, the project supported over 18,000 families annually, promoting the safety and well-being of survivors and children nationwide.
Expériences de violence dans les relations intimes et amoureuses au sein de populations LGBTQ+ (French only)
Expériences de violence dans les relations intimes et amoureuses au sein de populations LGBTQ+ (French only)
Interviews were held with LGBTQ2+ victims of intimate partner violence to document their needs. Collaborating with service providers, the project developed resources and training for professionals to prevent violence and enhance safety while addressing the unique challenges faced by LGBTQ2+ individuals.
Université du Québec à Montréal
SPARX : For positive romantic and intimate relationships
For positive romantic and intimate relationships
SPARX, a multi-component dating violence prevention program, was developed and tested for grades 9 to 10 students. Workshops taught youth, parents and school staff how to build healthy relationships and prevent violence. Delivered in French and English, the program was implemented in urban and rural schools across Quebec.
Université de Québec à Trois-Rivières
Project STEP : Supporting the transition into parenthood and parental engagement of adults who are survivors of child maltreatment Project profile
Supporting the transition into parenthood and parental engagement of adults who are survivors of child maltreatment
The Centre d'études interdisciplinaires sur le développement de l'enfant et de la famille developed group interventions for future parents who are survivors of child maltreatment. The program aimed to prevent intergenerational violence, improve parenting skills and enhance the physical and mental health of parents and their children in Quebec, fostering healthier family dynamics.
Saskatchewan - Regional
Gender Based Violence Project : Understanding gender-based violence in LGBTQ2S communities
Gender Based Violence Project :
Understanding gender-based violence in LGBTQ2S communities
Training was provided to health and social service providers in Saskatchewan to address gender-based violence in LGBTQ2S communities. Through education and mentorship, the initiative improved care quality and provider capacity to create safer, more supportive environments for LGBTQ2S individuals in the Prairies.
Saskatchewan - Regional
Provincial Association of Transition Houses and Services of Saskatchewan (PATHS)
NATO'WE HO WIN (the art of self-healing) Project profile
NATO'WE HO WIN (the art of self-healing)
A culturally relevant, arts-based program for Indigenous women survivors of family violence was delivered and tested in Moose Jaw, Regina and Prince Albert. The intervention fostered resilience, coping skills and community support, blending cultural traditions with trauma-informed healing practices.
Liard Aboriginal Women's Society
Tsʼídāne ā ́ʼnezen: Youth for Dignity
Tsʼídāne ā ́ʼnezen: Youth for Dignity
The Youth for Dignity project addressed relationship violence among youth in Watson Lake, an isolated First Nations community. Culturally relevant programming for grades 8 to 12 students increased awareness of gender-based violence while strengthening youth connections to Kaska culture. This initiative empowered students to foster healthier, dignity-based relationships.
World Health Organization (WHO)
WHO guidelines on parenting interventions to prevent child maltreatment and enhance parent-child relationships with children aged 0 to 17 years
WHO guidelines on parenting interventions to prevent child maltreatment and enhance parent-child relationships with children aged 0 to 17 years
Guidelines to strengthen caregiver and parenting programs were developed to prevent child maltreatment and violence. The project included strategies for training facilitators and reducing barriers to participation. It equipped health providers with tools to identify and deliver effective support for vulnerable families across Canada.
Metropolitan Action Committee on Violence Against Women and Children (METRAC)
The TransFormed Project : Better Health for Trans, Gender Diverse, and Two Spirit Survivors Project under Innovation Strategy
The TransFormed Project :
Health for Trans, Gender Diverse, and Two Spirit Survivors Project under Innovation Strategy
Two-Spirit, nonbinary and trans individuals in Toronto were engaged to address partner violence and barriers to care. Peer-led approaches and training for service providers were developed and tested, increasing awareness and improving equitable health and social supports for affected community members.
